title: Small Business Regulatory Flexibility Improvements Act
short_title: Small Business Regulatory Flexibility Improvements Act
plain_summary: This bill makes new rules to help protect small businesses from government regulations that could hurt them. Before government agencies create new rules, they must study how those rules might affect small businesses and look for ways to make the rules less harmful. Agencies also have to ask small businesses for their opinions before making major rules and regularly check if old rules are still causing problems. The bill makes it easier for small businesses to take agencies to court if they don't follow these protection rules, and it stops agencies from fining small businesses the first time they make paperwork mistakes.
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute by the Yeas and Nays: 13 - 12.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Small Business Regulatory Flexibility Improvements Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Clarification and Expansion of Rules Covered by the Regulatory Flexibility Act
sections.text: This section changes what rules government agencies must review for their impact on small businesses. It expands the definition of rule to include more types of government actions but keeps exceptions for veteran benefit rules and some specific financial rules. The section adds a new definition for economic impact that includes both direct effects on small businesses and indirect effects like extra costs or lost revenue that can reasonably be expected from a rule. It requires agencies to look at rules that help small businesses, not just rules that hurt them. The section also includes tribal organizations as small entities that must be protected. It requires agencies to do impact studies when they create or change land management plans for forests and public lands. The section includes certain tax recordkeeping rules that were previously excluded. It changes how small organizations are defined by using size standards set by the Small Business Administration, with special rules for local labor unions and allowing agencies to create their own definitions after consulting with small business advocates.

sections.title: Section 3  Expansion of Report of Regulatory Agenda
sections.text: This section requires agencies to include more information in their regulatory agenda reports. Agencies must describe which industry sectors will be most affected by new rules that will significantly impact many small businesses. Within three days of publishing their agenda in the Federal Register, agencies must put a plain language summary on their website. The Small Business Administration must also collect and display these summaries from all agencies on its website within three days.

sections.title: Section 4  Requirements Providing for More Detailed Analyses
sections.text: This section makes the analysis requirements much more detailed. When agencies first propose rules, they must provide detailed statements about why action is needed, the legal basis for the rule, how many small businesses will be affected, what compliance requirements exist, which federal rules might overlap or conflict, the total economic burden on small businesses, any unfair impacts on certain types of small businesses, and effects on small businesses' ability to get loans or credit. For final rules, agencies must provide detailed descriptions and explanations instead of basic ones, including detailed information about unfair economic impacts. Agencies must respond to comments about their initial analysis or their decision that no analysis was needed. They must post the complete analysis on their website and publish it or a summary in the Federal Register. Agencies can reference other required analyses to meet these requirements if they contain the same information. When agencies claim a rule won't significantly impact small businesses, they must provide a detailed factual and legal explanation with enough economic information to support their claim. All analyses must include specific numbers and quantities when describing effects, or if that's not possible, agencies must explain in detail why they cannot provide numbers.

sections.title: Section 5  Repeal of Waiver and Delay Authority; Additional Powers of the Chief Counsel for Advocacy
sections.text: This section gives the Chief Counsel for Advocacy of the Small Business Administration new powers and removes agencies' ability to delay or skip regulatory flexibility requirements. Within two hundred seventy days of this law passing, the Chief Counsel must create rules that govern how agencies comply with regulatory flexibility requirements. These rules can be changed later through public comment processes. Agencies cannot create their own supplemental rules without first consulting with the Chief Counsel. The Chief Counsel can now participate in agency hearings and decisions to inform agencies about impacts on small businesses, but cannot file appeals. The Chief Counsel can also comment on any agency notice requesting public comments, regardless of whether the agency must follow normal rulemaking procedures.

sections.title: Section 6  Procedures for Gathering Comments
sections.text: This section creates a detailed process for getting input from small businesses before agencies make major rules. Before proposing certain significant rules, agencies must notify the Chief Counsel for Advocacy and provide all materials and information about the rule, including potential economic impacts on small businesses. Tax-related rules and rules from independent agencies don't have to provide exact draft language. Within fifteen days, the Chief Counsel must identify small businesses or their representatives to get advice and create a review panel with employees from the Small Business Administration, the rulemaking agency, and for most agencies, the Office of Management and Budget. Within sixty days, the Chief Counsel must submit a report assessing economic impacts on small businesses, including effects on energy costs and startup costs, and discussing alternatives that would help small businesses. This report becomes part of the official rulemaking record, and agencies must explain what actions they took in response when they publish proposed rules. These procedures apply to rules that could have major economic effects including annual impacts of one hundred million dollars or more, major cost increases, significant effects on competition or employment, or significant impacts on many small businesses. The Chief Counsel can waive these requirements if they are impractical, unnecessary, or against the public interest. Small businesses can request copies of reports and materials, which agencies must provide within ten business days unless the information is protected from public disclosure.

sections.title: Section 7  Periodic Review of Rules
sections.text: This section requires agencies to regularly review their existing rules that significantly impact small businesses. Within one hundred eighty days of this law passing, each agency must publish a plan for reviewing rules that significantly impact many small businesses, regardless of whether they did the required analysis when the rule was made. The purpose is to determine if rules should continue unchanged, be modified, or be eliminated to minimize harm or maximize benefits to small businesses while meeting legal requirements. Agencies must review all existing rules within ten years and new rules within ten years of their publication. If agencies cannot complete reviews on time, they can extend the deadline by up to two years after notifying Congress and the Chief Counsel. Plans must include detailed sections on how agencies will reach out to and meaningfully include small businesses owned by women, veterans, and socially and economically disadvantaged individuals when conducting reviews. Agencies must contact small businesses and gather their input on existing rules. Each agency must submit annual reports to Congress, the Chief Counsel, and for most agencies, the Office of Management and Budget about their review results, including detailed explanations for any decisions about overlapping rules or cumulative impacts. When reviewing rules, agencies must modify or eliminate them to minimize harm or maximize benefits to small businesses as much as possible while meeting legal requirements. Agencies must consider factors like continued need for the rule, complaints from small businesses, complexity, overlap with other rules, cumulative economic impact, and changes in technology or economic conditions since the rule was made. Each year, agencies must publish lists of rules to be reviewed, ask for public comments about what should be included, explain why each rule significantly impacts small businesses, and request comments from the public and small business advocates about how rules are enforced.

sections.title: Section 8  Judicial Review of Compliance with the Requirements of the Regulatory Flexibility Act Available After Publication of the Final Rule
sections.text: This section makes it easier for small businesses to challenge agencies in court for not following regulatory flexibility requirements. Small businesses can sue about compliance issues as soon as a final rule is published, even if the rule is not yet a final agency action. Courts that would have jurisdiction over the rule if it were a final agency action can hear these cases. Small businesses have six months from when the final rule is published to file lawsuits, except when the final rule publication is the same as the final agency action date. The Chief Counsel for Advocacy can join these court cases to address agency compliance with regulatory flexibility requirements.

sections.title: Section 9  Jurisdiction of Court of Appeals Over Rules Implementing the Regulatory Flexibility Act
sections.text: This section gives federal appeals courts jurisdiction over final rules that the Chief Counsel for Advocacy makes about regulatory flexibility requirements. It adds the Office of Advocacy of the Small Business Administration to the list of agencies whose rules can be reviewed by appeals courts when the rules are about regulatory flexibility. It also allows the Chief Counsel to participate in court cases about agency compliance with administrative procedures including rulemaking and adjudication requirements.

sections.title: Section 10  Establishment and Approval of Small Business Concern Size Standards by Chief Counsel for Advocacy
sections.text: This section changes who can set the standards for determining what qualifies as a small business. The Administrator of the Small Business Administration can still set standards for Small Business Administration programs and the Small Business Investment Act. However, the Chief Counsel for Advocacy can now set these standards for all other federal laws. Any size standards not set by the Administrator must be approved by the Chief Counsel for Advocacy. Both the Administrator and Chief Counsel must ensure that size standards vary appropriately by industry. When someone wants to challenge a rule in court that includes size standards approved by the Chief Counsel, they can include the Chief Counsel as a party in the lawsuit.

sections.title: Section 11  Clerical Amendments
sections.text: This section makes technical formatting changes to existing law. It updates the definitions section to use clearer headings like Agency, Small business, Small governmental jurisdiction, and Small entity instead of numbered paragraphs. It changes the heading of one section to Incorporations by reference and certifications. It updates the table of contents to reflect new section titles including Quantification requirements and Additional powers of Chief Counsel for Advocacy. It removes outdated language about covered agencies and simplifies some paragraph formatting in sections about regulatory flexibility analysis requirements.

sections.title: Section 12  Agency Preparation of Guides
sections.text: This section updates requirements for agencies to prepare guides that help small businesses understand and comply with rules. Agencies have discretion in preparing guides but must write them in plain language that affected small businesses can understand, considering the subject matter and relevant laws. Agencies can prepare separate guides for different groups of similar small businesses and can work with small business associations to distribute guides. When developing guides, agencies must get input from affected small businesses or their associations. Agencies can prepare guides for individual rules or groups of related rules.

sections.title: Section 13  Comptroller General Report
sections.text: This section requires the Comptroller General of the United States to complete and publish a study within ninety days of this law being enacted. The study must examine whether the Chief Counsel for Advocacy of the Small Business Administration has the capacity and resources needed to carry out the duties assigned by this law and its amendments.

sections.title: Section 14  Waiver of Fines for First-Time Paperwork Violations by Small Businesses
sections.text: This section creates protection for small businesses from fines for their first paperwork violations. A first-time violation means a small business has violated an information collection requirement where they have not violated any similar requirement from the same agency in the previous five years. For first-time violations, agency heads cannot impose civil fines on small businesses, and they cannot consider violations of other agencies' requirements when making this determination. However, agencies can impose fines for first-time violations if the violation could cause serious harm to the public interest, would interfere with detecting criminal activity, involves tax laws, was not corrected within six months of written notification, or presents a danger to public health or safety. For violations that present danger to public health or safety, agencies may choose not to impose fines if the violation is corrected within twenty four hours of written notification to the business owner. When deciding whether to allow twenty four hours for correction, agencies must consider the nature and seriousness of the violation, whether the business made good faith efforts to comply and remedy the violation quickly, and whether the business got significant economic benefit from the violation. If an agency imposes a fine for a health or safety violation without allowing twenty four hours for correction, it must notify Congress within sixty days.
